ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for Chinese broccoli as a raw ingredient (FOODON_03311737). No specific entry exists for stir-fried kailan with garlic as a prepared dish; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers Chinese broccoli as a raw commodity. No prepared stir-fried dish code exists; closest_parent used for the primary vegetable ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for stir-fried kailan with garlic as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried kailan with garlic is approximately 200 g, presented as a plate of cooked leafy greens.

nutrient_source.title: Kailan, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for cooked kailan. Values for stir-fried kailan with garlic are adapted from HPB cooked kailan data, with a modest upward adjustment to fat and sodium to account for cooking oil and oyster sauce/salt used in stir-frying. Core macros are anchored to HPB cooked kailan per 100 g. Where HPB does not report a micronutrient, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Chinese broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt; FDC ID 170383) is used as a secondary reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for cooked leafy brassica vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried kailan with garlic in the University of Sydney GI Database. Non-starchy leafy brassica vegetables (e.g., broccoli, Chinese cabbage) are consistently reported with GI values in the range of 10–20 in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 15 is used as a defensible analogous estimate for cooked kailan, consistent with other cooked non-starchy brassica vegetables. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.0) / 100 = 1.2, where serving carbohydrate = 4.0 g/100g × 200 g serving = 8.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce and added salt during stir-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that restaurant-prepared stir-fried kailan with garlic may contain 250–350 mg sodium per 100 g due to oyster sauce and salt; requesting reduced sauce or a plain garlic stir-fry can substantially lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried kailan with garlic has a very low glycemic index (approximately 15) and provides only about 4 g of carbohydrate per 100 g, making it an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the high dietary fibre content further attenuates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and cardioprotective micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried kailan with garlic is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.5 g/100 g) and provides dietary fibre, potassium, folate, and vitamin C, all of which support cardiovascular health; the garlic component may additionally contribute to modest LDL-cholesterol reduction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Kailan with Garlic (蒜炒芥兰) is a classic Singaporean and Chinese vegetable dish made by quickly stir-frying fresh kailan — also known as Chinese broccoli or gai lan (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) — in a hot wok with aromatic garlic, cooking oil, and typically a small amount of oyster sauce or salt. The result is a vibrant green dish with a satisfying crunch, a mild bitterness characteristic of brassica vegetables, and a savoury garlic fragrance. Kailan is prized for its thick, glossy leaves and firm stems, which retain a pleasant bite even after cooking. The dish is a staple side vegetable in Singaporean hawker centres, Chinese restaurants, and home kitchens, commonly served alongside rice or noodle dishes. Nutritionally, stir-fried kailan with garlic is low in calories (approximately 52 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (approximately 4 g per 100 g), and provides a useful amount of dietary fibre (approximately 2.4 g per 100 g), vitamin C, vitamin A, folate, calcium, and iron. Its very low glycemic index (approximately 15) makes it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content is moderate due to oyster sauce and salt used in preparation, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat, supporting cardiovascular health. It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free, though it may contain molluscs if oyster sauce is used.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried kailan with garlic?
Is kailan the same as Chinese broccoli?
Can I eat kailan if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo cooked kailan data with secondary reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for micronutrients not reported by HPB. No exact HPB entry for stir-fried kailan with garlic as a prepared dish with garlic and oil exists; values are adapted with reasonable adjustments for cooking oil and seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked non-starchy brassica vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo cooked kailan and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170383, Chinese broccoli cooked). Fat and sodium values are adjusted upward from raw/plain-cooked values to account for stir-frying oil and oyster sauce/salt typical in restaurant preparation. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): kailan is a plant food and contains no vitamin B12 by nature — naturally zero. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): kailan is a plant food and does not naturally contain vitamin D — naturally zero. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are present in this minimally processed vegetable dish — naturally zero. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): kailan is a plant food and contains no cholesterol — naturally zero. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked non-starchy brassica vegetables (broccoli, Chinese cabbage); no direct GI measurement for kailan is available in the database. 7. Allergen note: oyster sauce, if used, introduces a mollusc allergen; some preparations use vegetarian oyster sauce (mushroom-based) which would be mollusc-free. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170383 as HPB does not report these micronutrients for this dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Cleaned food_name from 'V1. Stir-Fried Kailan with Garlic 蒜炒芥兰' to canonical 'Stir-Fried Kailan with Garlic'.
Added Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names for Singaporean cuisine compliance.
Populated per_100g nutrient values from HPB Singapore Foodo (adapted) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.
Added GI of 15 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for cooked non-starchy brassica vegetables.
Added health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance.
Added zero-value justifications in notes for vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Set ontology match types to 'closest_parent' or 'none' only; no invented codes used.
Added comprehensive llm_training section with natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters.
